speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This first interview with Mi Lew and Marie Lee Lew took place at their home on the edge of Moscow on November 20th, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: We don't fight that way, so once in a while the kid gets a bump on his head, and then the teacher believes us, and what he said is true. The teachers really don't bother us too much, they don't penalize us too much at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the Chinese folks live in the same, in one section in Walla Walla?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, they call it Chinatown. They lived on Fifth Street and Rose and Main Street, they had a little Chinatown there, had a little store there. More than one little store. I mean there was a whole chain of little stores. Yeah, small buildings around that one block. Then as a general rule, the Chinese stores are, you know, you're a Smith, then you're a Smith, they've got a little Smith store, you know, and then the Jones, you've got a little Jones store, you see, because the Chinese considered, if you're a Jones, you and the Smith are not the same family, so the Smiths got, all the Smiths go and patronize the Smiths in the store, and the Jones store goes to Jones store, see. But they, you know, that's where they usually are, see, and so they do business that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many different family groups would there have been?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I don't mean exactly. Oh, oh, oh, they usually, at that time there was three stores there, four, five, six, seven, seven stores at that time. And years ago, you know, years ago in China, when, ages ago, then there used to be a family three, four families, when the people were in tribes at that time, you see. The little tribes are just like the, like I say, little tribes, then they unite with the other, some bigger tribes, so they form one group, see, and then they protect themselves, that's where they are. Then we are the Lu family, you see, Lu family, we're a smaller tribe, see, and so therefore we had three, you see, Liu Gang, Jiang, Jiang, there's four tribes coming with us, see. Four families. Yeah, four families. Coming to one tribe. Yeah, yeah, coming to one, one, one group, see, and all the people now, like I said, run that little store now, all those four families would come in patternized, that one store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long, about, would it be centuries or just a short time for families to be together?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, centuries, oh, far, exactly. Centuries, centuries, centuries. This has been going back, way back into the, oh, I would say, gone back to the time of Christ, bring it here, see, they've been that, when they used to be nomads, you know, and they just tribes around, see, and the Chinese had three kingdoms, you know, you have to read Chinese history, I don't know that much about it, Chinese had three kingdoms at that one time, see, and then, you know, and they used to form into three different groups, see, and then, you know, that's the way they... And then three kings in... In the whole China, three kings, they call it, yeah, but you have to go to Chinese history, really, I...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Three kings in three countries, then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah, in China now, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you supposed to marry within the family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it within these four families or were you supposed to marry outside?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, you can marry within those four families, but a Li or a Lu could not marry their own family. Oh, I see, sure. Yeah, they don't, see, they consider...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But marry one or the other three?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, the other three, or you can marry somebody else, but you never marry a brother or sister, in other words, because...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you can marry outside, outside the group?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, you can, you can marry outside the group. You have to go farther than... Yeah, but... Would there be thousands of generations, like the cousins, you know, well, cousins and cousins, over here, they don't marry either, but were even a thousand cousins away, you know, we still don't marry, see, even have no blood whatsoever among, but if you are a Li... You can not marry Li. Well, you are a Li from way back in the time of Christ, so now, you know, the brother and sister, oh, their blood has disintegrated, you know, into other marriages already, so there is no such thing as... So it's a lot farther than what the American people call cousins, you know, more, it'd be hundreds of cousins, instead of first cousin, second cousin, third cousin, you go back hundreds of cousins, and there'd be thousands of Li's, you still don't marry a Li, don't marry a Li, who is about 6 ,000 miles away, still don't marry another Li, usually they don't. I mean... Yeah, I think the main purpose of that, you know, the Chinese people aren't, like I said, the majority of the people aren't educated, they don't research into that thing, and during those centuries, you know, when there was tribe, and I would consider that the government, you know, the Chinese people said, in order to set it, right now you have research back there and see whether blood counts and all that, but all this other stuff, they say a Li, she never married a Li. Chinese who do not have too much people that, you know, who already have the kind of disease that where you don't stop, you know, when you cut, you don't bleed, there are very, very few of them, we don't have hardly any of those types, see, because that's one reason they just, you know, they just marry... They have pure blood. Yeah, pure blood, and they're not respected. Customs usually have some kind of reason. Oh yeah, that's reason, except, you know, you see that most of the people are agriculture people, they're ignorant people, you make one law like that and everybody follows it, see, instead of going through doctors and then, you know, doing this and that, you know, in that way, see, and that's the way, I think that's what happens here. The little that I've read about Chinese civilization seems to indicate that the Chinese people had a very highly advanced science and... Oh yeah, they did. Way, way back before the West developed that time.

speaker: Mi Lew
words: Oh yeah, they did, they did, they had a way back, oh yeah, they did at that time, but they had a really, you know, they had advanced so much, then they, you know, they got to the top of where they had machinery to do some of the work, but when they had that machinery to do some of the work, they take the work out of the other guy, and they say, whoa, whoa, whoa, what are these other people going to do?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So, so they kind of stagnant for a while, when the king, for a while, one king burned all the books, you know, burned all the books and let them get there so the people would start over again, so they wouldn't be so, you know, so smart that they'd get to hold the other people down, see, they had it one time that way, see, they burned all the books, but they say that king was no good because he burned all the books, because he was, at that time, the people was scientifically very smart at that time, see, they could not operate, no, they can, they what they called in the Chinese at that time, they said, they had one doctor that said he can cut a chicken's head off and change his feet, and he said, and all that stuff that way, you know, that's just a word of the mouth, see, he was so skillful and he cut these things that way, but you know, Chinese are not very good in patting it or something, you know, writing it down, see, they tell the family, you know, they tell the family, you know, what it is, all right, they're very jealous and they keep the secrets to themselves, to themselves, yeah, etc., brought it out, even if you got a secret in the family, you keep the secret in the family, it's fine, but they don't like to tell it out too much, see. Well, if you talk about the four, like the four families that were along with the Lee family, would all those families share pretty much, or would they share, oh yeah, yeah, oh yeah, yeah, yeah, like a big family, that's all, I think part of it, it's to protect themselves. It's because they're the smaller ones, the smaller ones, with the Lees and the Aims and the Wongs and the Chins and all that, they're a big family, they don't need any, they don't need to group together, so these other little, you know, like the Smiths and the Jones, they're a big family anyway, shoemakers, you know, and bakers and all that, you know, but the little ones that are odd names, you know, like in the American family, they're so small and they have so few members that they have to group themselves in order to fight, to protect themselves, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the names of the families that were with the Lee family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, with the Lew, it's the Lew family, Lew, Jie, and the Lee II, they had a group not in this group, but they had another group here. What is it, Lew, Jie, Lew Kang Jie Jie,

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: yeah, Lew Kang Jie Jie, the Lew Kang Jie Jie, that's the time the three kingdoms, that's the time when China was divided into three kingdoms, but Lew Kang Jie Jie was considered one kingdom, say one kingdom, and Lew was supposed to be the king or the older brother, you know, the Chinese are very, what do you call it that way, when the older one rules, the oldest one, older brother, the rules, he rules, just like a, I suppose as an Indian, the same thing, you hire, get a chief, the chief rules, they don't make any difference, you know. The oldest brother. The oldest boy in the family, whatever he says, rules after the father goes. Yeah, well whatever he says, usually, in other words, he's the head of the family, in other words.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does it pass on to his oldest son or to the next oldest brother after he dies?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, usually the Chinese family divides, you know, and then when you have, what do you call it, now you have two brothers, then your brothers, you're split there, see, you're split there, the brother, the other brother was the head of his family, the oldest brother was the head of his family, see, but the father of the two, you know, what he got, he really divided between the two sons, see, so they had no trouble in that respect, see, but the Chinese years ago, you know, but when the father owes the debt, you know, the son is supposed to pay. Have to pay it. See, it didn't like, here, you know, when your old debt don't pay. At the prior year, you don't have to pay here, see. Yeah, but the Chinese customs, like I say, everything goes to the son, goes to the children, I mean, usually the son, usually the daughter, they consider a daughter is married to somebody else, somebody else's son can look after her, see, that attitude, see, but as a general rule, the daughter don't have to pay the father's debt, but the son do, see, the son, if the father is the bad father, yeah, if the father has to be a bad, you know, drunkard or gambler or something, but then the son will usually pay the father's debt, you know, see, that way, see. That's a lot of responsibility. That is a lot of responsibility. They considered, you know, a father had raised your children, had raised your children, had given you the life, given you life to your child, you know, you give your life to your child, and they figured that you owe your life to your parents. They figured that you owe your life to your parents, therefore, so they figured, after all, life is a cycle, life is a cycle, so if the father supports the son when he's small, and it feels, the Chinese customers feel that the son should support the dad when he gets old. There's not too many Chinese in a nursing home, not very, very few. And he wants to be able to take care of himself. Yeah, yeah, there's very, very few. They usually like to take care of their own family, because even a family, you know, even, I would say, even said, Lloyd would like to come home, but nobody now takes care of, to that extent, see. So, in other words, we're getting more modern, see. But, technically, Chinese, you go right through, I think you go down to San Francisco, although there are not very many outside the world, there are not too many in the nursing home, they feel that the family should, that the children should take care of the older folks. If the wife or daughter -in -law, son, you know, able to take care of them, then they are taken care of right in the home. But, you know, if they are unable to take care of them, then they have to go to a nursing home, which is not too often. Now, like Ted said, his wife is gone, and he, when his wife is living, his sister is there, he stays home, he, except when he gets real sick, and then he goes to the hospital, and then he goes to the nursing home for a few days, until he doesn't have to see the doctor so often, and then he comes home to recuperate. But now his wife is gone, his sister is too old to take care of him, he cannot, she cannot give her a shot for diabetes, you know. I mean, even once a present day, you have to give him a shot. Well, anyway, she's unable to do this for him, so for his own health, for his own sake, it's better that he is there, and then the grandson, the grandson has five children of his own, from one up to six years old, you see. Well, they try to run the restaurant, so they have no time, and the grandson and his wife doesn't have time enough to take care of him at home. I can see that it's a lot more important than it is to average Americans. Oh yes, much more important. Because this society, the kids go, you know, the Oprah's go to the home, and the kids try to set it up so they don't have to pay more than they have to. And try to let the government take care of them. And it's bad, a lot of times, because I've seen a lot of it, trying to talk to old timers who are in the homes, because so many of them just really go down the hill once they get to the hospital. It's a whole different world than they ever lived in before. That's right, that's right. If they were home, you see, they had their own people there, they had their grandchildren there, at least on their good days, if they're not too sick, you know, they can enjoy what's going on in family life, you see. And then that way, where they can live longer, and they have a desire to live longer. This way, if they're off to one place, you know, in the nursing home. Some of them are good, and not too bad, but some of them are terrible, you know, in the nursing home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When we talked a little about the people coming from Canton, and that's where you're, you were born there, but you came over very young, right? How old were you?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I was six, five and a half, six year old. You know, six year old in Chinese is actually five years old. Because on the day they're born, they're one year old already. You see, they figure, the Chinese figure this way, they've been conceived nine months ago, you see. So on the day they're born, they're nine months old anyway, so they call it a year old. Oh, that's nice. Well, and on top of that too, on top of that too, the Chinese people, as a general rule, as a nation itself, you know, does not celebrate birthday by themselves too much. They usually celebrate New Year, see. New Year, the New Year they figure everybody add another year unto themselves, see. So that's the way they, in New Year, in Chinese New Year, in China, they close up a week, a week and ten days, they store it, everybody close up, you know. Everybody close up, and the only thing you can buy is a little fresh vegetable and a little fresh meat. Outside you can't buy a suit of clothes or anything at all. Everything is just closed up. Everybody, no matter where you go, you go back home usually. The people that work in town now, in the city, they go home for a week and ten days, they call it New Year, see. Then everybody have that way, they call it birthday at that time, another year. In other words, another year, another year older. That's the way they go. And the nation in general, that's the way they go. That's another year to their age. So, if you're born July, you're one year old, next Chinese New Year, you're two years old. So the one that have to add on lots more years, you know, the one that born on the 31st of December, the one year already, the next day was only two days old, they're two years old on the Chinese New Year day. But my mother happened to be very lucky. She was born on New Year's Day, so she's only got one year added on to hers, you see, so she's always one year to that advantage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in Walla Walla, and there was Chinese community there, did they celebrate Chinese New Year's Day? Very active?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, they did. Very active. Very active. In Honolulu, they did. They still celebrate in Honolulu. In Honolulu, they're active. In any town where they have a big Chinese cafe, they celebrate. Yeah, San Francisco, or whatever, big Chinese town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Walla Walla, when you were just a kid, do you remember what the Chinese New Year's were like there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, the Chinese New Year's were, first, they shoot firecrackers. You know, they don't shoot firecrackers. At the New Year's Eve, they go on what they call New Year's Eve now, the going of the year, the O year. Going on the O year, well, just before that, they have a great big meal, you know. They usually have a whole - On New Year's Eve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On New Year's Eve, they have a great big meal there, okay?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: On New Year's Eve, and then everybody comes and eats the meal, and then about 10 years of the year, 12 o 'clock, and then the night now. They go and put up some fruit and chicken and what you call it, they go worship the coming of the New Year. The time between 12 and the time between the New Year and the O year, going on the New Year, coming. They have set up a little big table with fruit and Chinese goodies and what you call it, and then they kind of worship, pray the New Year coming in, the O year going by. In the New Year, now it's going to be a good year coming now, and everybody's going to be prosperous. Then the next day, or next three, four days, they have great big meals. Then everybody, you know, you don't have to be invited to go in the store. You go in there and get something to eat, you know. They invite you to eat. Then they usually give children, you know, children. The Chinese consider children, you know, as persons not married, even if you're 25 years old. If you're not married, you're still a child, see. The only time you're not a child is when you're married, see. Then when you're married, you're supposed to be at this family, see. But most of them, then they give a little package of red with a little red envelope with a little money in it, you know, and then they give it to, whenever they see children, they give it to the children. A good luck sign is a red paper. It's a red, always red, is a good luck. And white is bad luck. That's why they use that in funerals. They wear white robes for funerals like that. Any, in China when they have a bride come in, you know, wearing a bride or gown, it's all red and gold, but predominantly red. That's a good luck color. Anything that's happy, birth of a child, you know, a happy occasion, engagement or marriage or anything like that, it's always, they got lots of red around. Well, you see, I think the red part is that the Chinese consider, you know, for the human body, the human body, because the American people consider heart is the important part of the human body, but the Chinese people consider blood is the most important part of the body. The Chinese got herbs and what you call it, they're trying to build the blood up and trying not to, you know, to, you know, try to, you know, to make everything one food. You got to have good blood, you know, you got to have good blood to do it. But you see, I think that's the reason that they have red as a symbol for, you know, good, for good, because blood is considered Chinese as more important than heart is. In a way, they're, in a way, I think they are right in a way, you know, it kind of, you know, just, although as a unit, blood and heart has to go together, see, without one or the other, it doesn't function, actually it doesn't function. But, you know, I noticed the other day we went out and killed a pig, you know, but, you know, the first thing that the farmer did to shoot the pig, knocked the pig down, he opened his throat out so the blood would rip, see, so therefore, in order to stop the heart, what you call it, you can have a heart go without blood, the pig can't eat it. So, I said it goes through the whole body. I know, I know, but, you know, the Chinese considered blood is more important than heart, always did, because the Chinese people hate to get shot, stick a needle, draw blood out, they hate to do that. They hate to lose any blood. Yeah, they hate to lose blood. They consider it a very, what do you call it, they do. They would hardly ever sell any of their blood. Yeah, they just hate that. Unless they're very poor, you know, and that's a good way of getting money, well then they would do it. So, on New Year's Day, you see, children have a, you know, they know how to get a little extra money, you know, so they go around, of course, their own families, you know, and then their friends, you know, they go around because wherever they go, they always get a little, receive a little package of money wrapped in red paper on these first few days of the new New Year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you were in Walla Walla and growing up, were there, were most of the families there just the men and their kids, and most half of the wives too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, the men, the men, the men, yeah, the mostly Chinese, I think there were just two women there, that's all in there. There was 300 and some men, just two women there, I think it was, somebody's wife and just two families.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And most of the men were in the farming world?

speaker: Mi Lew
words: Yeah, most, well they used to be railroad a year ago, see, but when the railroad quit and then they got into the farming business, they got into the agriculture, you know, the vegetable farming, see, they get a couple acres and then they go plant vegetables and then go sell the vegetables.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that when most of the women?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, at that time, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of them work in the orchards too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, not too many work in the orchards, no, not too many orchards. There isn't too much orchard in Walla. That's true. Yeah, there was an apple orchard, Langdon Apple orchard, that was considered big, but they have, you know, they never did, there are too many Chinese.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did the kids come to America instead of staying in China with the mothers? Why did they come with the fathers?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, they come with the father because, you see the fathers came over here in the railroad at that time. They only use 18 boys. Yeah, they took boys, yeah. Because they can take care of the boys easier than having a girl along. You see, they come to America because it's America, they consider it's a land of opportunity. They come over here, one reason is they can make one dollar, then they get five dollars back there. But what Americans change is you get one, you save one American dollar, you get five dollars back in Chinese money. At that time, the five dollars will actually buy more stuff than the American one dollar.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You know, actually, back there, they're living, it'll be like, what do you call it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So therefore, you see, at that time, when I came, the railroad was finished.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The railroad was finished, the Chinese were just kind of converging one and the other a little bit, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And so therefore, right after the railroad, I think the government won't let the Chinese come in anyway, the women come in anyway. They won't let the women, no, no, no, in the first place, the Chinese government didn't want their wives to go.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Then after that, when the Chinese government don't mind so much, when the boxes are rising and then they open up the, you know, the Western truth to China, then of course, then the American government didn't want the Chinese people to move in here, see? So, you know, the gates just kind of close one end or the other, see? But the boys, we had to come in, most of the Chinese, a lot of the Chinese come in as a general rule, have to fake a name or something, you know, in order to say a citizen or something like that, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's why a lot of the Chinese people are that way, you know, through that, because they only allow either 111 or 118 a year. A year, they only allow that much. For any country in the world, they allow thousands a year, we allow 118 a year.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So they have a film in order that I, my father is so -and -so, see, in order to do that, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That wasn't a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the time when you came in, was it the same way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, I was, I came in as a citizen's son. I mean, I'm not a citizen's son, I mean a merchant's son. My father was supposed to be doing business here as a merchant, and therefore I just came in as a son to go to school. I did, I went to school, I went through all the years in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that his first time here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, my father's been here before, yeah. My father was here before, he was in the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When would you guess he was first here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, he was 18 -something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He worked on a railroad building in the northwest, do you think?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I don't know too much, but my uncle knows it, yeah, those guys did, yeah. 18 -something, then my father went back, I think, 18 -something, then he went back to China for, must be 10, 15 years, I think. Then he decided to come back, see, that's all.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And when he was in China during that time, then he got married then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, he got married, I was born, see, I was 16.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: I imagine when he come over, did he come over with his father too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no, no, no. He just come over as a young man. Yeah, a young man, yeah, the one that labored. He didn't do mining, he worked in the railroad. No, the railroads, yeah, no, the mining was afterwards. Yeah, you know, the people, Chinese people are not very good miners because they never had mining in China that way, see, and so they came over as a railroad worker, see, mostly as a laborer, see, because you can tell them that, you know, they ditch it and stuff, that's fine, see, but as a miner we have no experience, but after the railroad was built, then they have to do something, then they got the idea, they go out, you know, they go pack gold, you know, and they got more jobs, you see, so they have to do something else, to create a job for themselves, to get something to eat out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that when your father came back to China for the first time that he had done well as far as, did he have, had he made money actually here to bring back, was he in good shape before he was here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, he was, I would say, he, well you see, you know, you don't have to do very much, do better than those people in China, you see, the people in China are practically, you know, they live from hand to mouth, they're pretty like India, you know. Like three -quarter acres of land maybe, you know. Like in India, you know, what you call it, but of course he worked hard and I think it may be, I would say he probably, say maybe five or six hundred dollars and he worked probably for five, six years or something like that, and then, then, then, then, then he was, I think he worked longer than that, in fact, he came over the first time, he worked longer than that because my mother is quite a bit older than, than, than, than, I mean I imagine he was older, I didn't talk to him so much, of course at that time I was young and so I don't talk to him how, how, how old he is. I think you said that your father didn't marry until after he was thirty, thirty -some years old or something like that. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, see, he, he's quite, my mother was quite a bit younger than, than, than, than, than. So, he, he saved about five or six hundred dollars. Yeah, I imagine, yeah, he did. Of course that's five or six hundred dollars more than anybody he back there had, you know, at the time when he went back, you see. So, so he went back, you know, and, and, and of course we have a little land back there too, and, and of course, and then my grandfather had, had had to take, to, to, to the family and vegetables and raise a few, enough food for, for, for the family, see. And then, then he, he was doing, he was doing fairly good in, in, in China. He was making a fairly living, I would say not, not good here, but I mean living, yeah, living. It was farm, it was all farming that he was making. Yeah, he was farming, and then he was in business too. He was in, in, in, in, in, in the drugstore business here in, in, in China for a while. Then, then of course he was partnering in it, and so, and the drugstore business did real good of course, but, but at that time it didn't pay off very much, but I mean, during the, what, what made him come back was, you know, at that time they had a lot of robbers. They, they, a whole bunch of bandits come in and take everything out of your house, you know, cleaning your right house, see, and then, then the, Living in the village there at that time. They cleaned up everything. They just, they just took it off, and then they had to buy new furniture and all that, and the, the, the, the, the second time he got the, the banner of a coblet robbed, and then he hadn't got it anymore, and so, so he came back to work, and the last time he came back he worked 18 years, and then I was a boy then, of course I, I went through school from, from the great first grade. The second time he come back he, he brought you with him. Yeah, yeah, that's it, that's the time. He only came home just twice, yeah. About five, five or six years old then.

In China, where we are, there's no winter, except there, you know, doesn't get cold like this here, you see, we're living south. It grows like hallelujah, it grows from here all the time, you know, it's that way, but, but you can't plant rice continuously because at that time there's no fertilization in which you add to it in order to, to otherwise, yeah, one crop, yeah, see, so one crop of rice a year, then then you grow other vegetables, you can grow, you can grow, uh, you know, on the same soil, on the same soil, because you plow it up, see, then you grow, then, then, then, then on top of that, wherever we are, right by our, our, our, our land, there's a stream there, see, there's a loose stream there, that the creek, you know, it's a little bit bigger than the one we got out here at the university, going through the university, but then, then, then, because during the, the, the, the, the, the, the rice, during the time that the rice needs water, we take the water out of the creek, flood the rice fields, see, but, but in the fall of the year, in the fall of the year when we don't need so much water, we, the, the, everybody around the, they dam the, the creek up, they dam the creek up, and then, then, the, the, I mean, they dam the creek up, I mean, due to, to, the rice fields during the summer, then in the winter, they lift the dam up, and then they put a net there to catch all the fish that, in, in that dam, see, then they divide it amongst the, the, the group that, you know, that farms around there, see, and so we get enough fish to, you know, to, to, to eat with the, with the rice, that's how you call it, and then we raise the pig, raise the chicken, so we, you know, like I said, we kill the, the pig, and then, and then the chicken, that way, for example. How important was the water buffalo for a family to... Family, they never eat a water buffalo, that's what I mean.

words: No, but I mean, what, what was it used for?

words: Oh, the buffalo used, the, the, the, the, the, the, the, the, the, the, the, the, the buffalo is a worker, like a tractor is. You know. Yeah, he's a tractor, he's a, he's a, he's a, really the, the, the, the, the, the second one to the, the, to the, to the... To the man. To the man, yeah, because he, he, he ploughs all the soil, and, you know, he does all the pollen, he does everything, the way he calls it, you know, and, and, really, it, it, it's, it's, that's the reason most of the Chinese people don't, don't eat, eat, eat, eat beef at all, see, that, that's the reason, because it's a, it's a working animal, but I know my grandfather, you know, usually, the year that we have a, a good crop, you know, we have a good crop, you know, and, you know, and then, then, then, then, then, then, then, then, my grandfather usually fixed something, you know, fixed something nice for the water buffalo to have, see, because we had such a good year, and the buffalo did so hard with work, and so, so, we really fixed him something real nice for the buffalo to have, to, to, to have, too, see. And also, for the people that don't have a buffalo, can afford to buy a buffalo, then the man himself will have to do the plowing. Yeah, you know, you have to dig it out by himself. They'll have to hog, they have to hitch him instead of the buffalo, and then he'll have to pull it. Well, like, you know, old days here, you know, we still see pictures, like the Waltons, you know, some of those old pictures where the man, a poor man still have to, to harness himself, you know, and, and go do some plowing. We're in South America that way, down in Peru, down in South America. So, whoever owns a water buffalo is considered a pretty good, a pretty good, you see. And so, that's one thing I imagine his father will be better off than the next person. And that might be because he came here. Yeah, he got his money to buy a water buffalo. And he has enough money to buy a water buffalo so he can work this water buffalo, you know, instead of working him, well, harnessing himself, why he can work behind the buffalo, let the buffalo do the harder part of the work, and he does the brain part, if that's what you call it.

They got a bigger, a bigger, you know, bigger voice than you have. Well, did, did you, I wonder if they tried to protect the places if they, if they had the guards for the, you know, for the, the stores in the village or that kind of thing. At home, you see. They, they, they take out the furniture. Yes. They, they, they, they, they take off, well, you know, if you had a cow and you have a, a, a pig or two and then you have chicken, they clean that, they clean all that out, it takes a while in order to get that back, you know, see, you know, it wasn't, it wasn't a store, no, they don't, they don't rob stores. They take a while off the lawn, you know. Yeah, they don't rob a whole lot. That's the only thing you have. As a general rule though, if you're poor, they don't rob you, you know. You have to take the money, they don't have to take the money, they don't take anything, But if you're doing well, you got a little village that maybe 15 families or so, you know, you're doing so well, then the others are not doing too well, then they figure out something with it, so you get cut down, you're their side.

words: Well, this is the question that I have, is how would your family, like, how would they have felt having to have separate like that, your father coming here, your mother staying behind, and you coming, and other kids staying, would that be a, did they look at that as a real hardship?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, that's a, because, you know, the Chinese people consider it a man is the head of the family. He's supposed to provide, you know, for the family. See, if they can provide things for the family, the wife don't mind to stay home by herself or with her child until he can, you know, until he come back there. Lots of Chinese come over here to the United States that married not too long, they come over here and they died over here, you know, and then their wives still stay back there in China. Still waiting, you know, there are a lot of them do that. They're very, like I say, that the older Chinese are very faithful, that they don't chase around with anybody at all. They just stay there because their grand, you see, once a wife marries the man's side, then the mother -in -law, you know, it looks after the wife, see. No matter what it is, the mother -in -law, the wife will always have something to eat because mother -in -law, if mother -in -law eats, the wife gets something to eat, you know, that way, you see, and then in other words, the father will provide, the grandparents will provide for the wife, see. But then, of course, when the son do well, come back, then everybody goes and do well, see, that's what they do. They don't like it, of course, they don't like it. They don't like that.

But with a wife, you can't do that.

Until they were able to become citizens then. And then, during that whole time, the only exceptions to let Chinese people in were if there was a certain class, for certain reasons or students. Of course, a lot of them went to school, and a lot of them now in Vinson, in Seattle, and in Portland, and all those, there's some in businesses. Then, of course, a lot of those younger people married, and some of them went to China to marry and bring their wives over. When I first came over, like I say, I was the only Chinese kid. Of course, the older men, all the older men, they liked children.

words: You have to, what I mean is that recreation like that would be important, something to, Yeah, that's what you do, yeah, there's nothing to do Let go Yeah, that's right Well, that's it, see, and then the more you play, or especially the more you lose, you think, well, gee, You play one more, get it back, see One more time, I get it back, see, well, that's how it goes What, was there a special particular card game that they played, or, Oh, they, Was it Chinese, or, The Chinese played, They played American -like poker No, they played, they got a Chinese diamond, you know, a little diamond, you know, what you call it, a little, And then they could play the fan tan, they called it fan tan, with the four squares there, And then they take a handful of beans, except they use buttons, what you call it, they got little Chinese buttons, They take a handful and then they leave it there, then you guess, then if you buy, if you put your money on the corner, You get double your money, if there's a one, two, three, four now, if there's a one and two, Either one of them, you double your money if you buy it on the first corner, see, But if you buy it only on one, if it comes out at one, then you get three times your money, see, So that's the way they gambled, that type, see, they're just guessing, you know, they're trying to, They just take a handful, they just take a handful of beans and set it there in the middle, then what you call it, And then they count it four by four, you know, then they count it, what's the odd one, whatever it is, the last one, The last square, yeah, the last two, three, whatever it is, and then you win, see, that's all they give, They play that mostly because they consider that the fan tan, they call it, And of course they do have this, what do you call it, two, this, what's it called in Las Vegas, they call it,

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Gino, Gino, Gino, Gino, Gino, I think it's something like that, They got spots, eighty letters, it's a Chinese book, they got eighty different words, eighty different words, On one page, on one page, you see, in other words you get eighty numbers all on one page, Then they roll something, they open twenty at eighty, they open twenty of the eighty letters, they mix it all up and open, Then you're supposed to find out which one of the twenty that they open, you buy it down there, Then they pay, if you open I think five or something like that, then you double your money, Then you stick, you get all ten of them, you make a lot of money, You pay to tell how much you've bought, how much you put in, you don't guess, Well, out of the eighty words, you mark so many, you're allowed ten, You can buy as many as you want to, but you get less, Oh, I see, well, they usually do ten spots, you mark, well, the Chinese people use words, but the American people use numbers, And then someplace else, they got some kind of roll up, something, and they turn at them, to get these twenty come out, They turn to someplace else, They got a little air tube, like a golf ball, like a ping pong ball, like a ping ball with numbers, They blow it, then they circulate, the balls circulate, then they open a slot, you see, and they drop one of the balls out, The ball has got numbers on it, and when they get numbers, they open those twenty, If you happen to hit so many of those twenty, the more you hit, the more money you buy, It depends on, if you buy twenty five cents, you only get so much, but if you buy five dollars, you get more,

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Stops at the truck garden, see, there used to be twenty five Chinese gardens there, each garden get about eight or ten people working there, They buy food, Chinese food, you know, Chinese canned goods, rice, and stuff like that, Chinese storage, supply it to these gardens, So your father was a store owner? A merchant, yeah, then of course, some of the partners wanted to get into it, so he had to go outside and run a restaurant, see, afterwards, too, Was he being supported, did he get money from partners to start into the business, when he started running the store? Did he have enough capital to start?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, they all get together, actually a partnership to start with, actually a partnership to start with, no, nobody had enough money to start with, Because the Chinese people, they carried those gardeners for a year, they carried from the time until the crop comes, until the crop comes to sell there, until the harvest, They carried no interest, no way, because they did whatever they wanted, they come and get it, whatever they wanted, they come and get it, carry it, and then they figured it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was your father, was he in partnership with the same other families that were moved?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Chinese, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, they were.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Hello?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: No, she isn't, I think she's downtown someplace.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You want to leave a message?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Renee?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Okay, you bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, was each truck, was the truck garden, the people who would be in the garden would be from the same family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, not necessarily, no, not necessarily, no, and sometimes too, and sometimes they, if there happened to be a poor year, the farmer can't pay all of it, and they have to care for two years sometimes. Yeah, yeah, because they all work hard, they work hard, they couldn't make enough to pay it, so you have to carry them another year. Chinese people do do that. And no interest or anything, they just go ahead and carry them all that way. I imagine the partners all put out so much money to buy this. Yeah, buy the stocks from Seattle or San Francisco. San Francisco or Vancouver, you know, from the coast, where the ships come into the coast and then they sell it to these stores. In Spokane, they used to have a Chinatown too, they call it the China Alley, Chinese Alley. One alley is Chinatown and the other alley is a Japanese town, and they would be having some Chinese stores just like that. They have only there is one big block in Walla Walla, but in Spokane we have an alley between Trent and Main. They tore it down. They tore it down, yeah, they tore it down. And now there's no more Chinatown. But it was much bigger than the one in Walla Walla. No, Walla Walla was bigger. Yeah, because you see in Spokane, the only people that's in is the few that are in the restaurant. That's all, Spokane never was an agriculture. More people in Walla Walla, Chinese people in Walla Walla than there are in Spokane. I would have thought that, but... You see, this way too, Chinese in Walla Walla, immigrants come in, you work in the garden, Chinese, you work, but in Spokane you can't, you go to work in the restaurant, you gotta have a little English in order to work in there. But the garden, you don't have to know one word. You can just go down and they can tell you to plant onion, they show you how to plant onion, you can go sit there and plant onion, because they have a few that know how to seed it, and know how to irrigate it, and that's all you do is harvest it and plant it, and that's all. Were these gardens, were they owned in common, or did one man... No, they all went together, they all went to work. They rented from... Co -op.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They rent who?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They rent the land from the American people, because they can't own land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They couldn't own land?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, they can't own land. When you're not a citizen, you cannot own land. They own land, so how can you see, so they can't, they just lease it. But then what he's trying to say is that these groups on each farm, it's co -op, and they all work together, they all eat together in a small... In the same food, and then they divide it. When the fall comes, the harvest, and they divide it. All the expenses paid, and they divide it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you said maybe what, 15 men in one farm?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I saw some big ones there, but a lot of them have about, most of them have about six or eight. But there are some big ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And these six or eight, they didn't have to be from the same family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no, they're friends, they're usually friends. You can work together, you know, over here, you know, they come over here, you know, and not only families is grouped together then, see, you know, outsiders are in too, because... They're mostly friends, because you're living together all year round, so you gotta be, you know, you gotta be able to work together. You have to be friends enough, because if one guy can go to work at five o 'clock in the morning, you have to get to work at five o 'clock in the morning, you can't say I'm gonna sleep until six in the morning, because you're gonna have trouble, see. See, so, you're usually a friend. You all have to have the same ideas and the same goal and the same ambition too, you know. One of them, or two of them, cannot be lazy ones and sleep a couple hours later. And then probably... Go ahead, let her... And probably the one that has to cook the meal, evening meal, maybe he can be off the field, you know, an hour, two hours earlier, you know, to cook the meal for the group of workers, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So probably like one man would take care of all the cooking?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, usually. Or maybe, well, you know, arrangements, it's like two students, you know, or maybe one of them would cook for one week or another one cook for... But usually, one of the men like to cook or know a little bit more about cooking than the other guy, you know. And probably one can do better out in the field and one can do better doing the cooking and maybe a third one can do the dishes, you know, and clean up afterwards. I wonder how big one of the plots would be, how many acres would... In truck farming, you don't need too much, but I'm not sure how much. But we have a friend that's down there now, originally, they have 40 acres and they do real well. And in fact, that 40 acres when he do the truck farming, he has to have a lot of equipment. And you see, in the old days, they don't have any equipment except just like your own little household garden to do the weeding and things like that, you know, all by hand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are these friends of yours? Had they been there in doing truck farming in the way of the early days?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, his father and mother were. They've been there, the father had been there for 40, 50 years.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I wonder if he started like Mr.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: B's father did. No, I think he came in a little bit later, I'm not sure.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Something going on there?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: No. This is our son -in -law. He has this trailer put down there. Sometimes, you know, things might be in trouble or something. He has to check into it. So, do you think... You see, in their 40 acres, and I think they rent another 10 or 20, they have lots of equipment. They have two, three tractors and diggers and everything. And then they sell it to the canneries. And their crops are as big as...well, one of their biggest crops is onions, of course, the dry onions.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Do you want to tell them to come down a little bit later?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: I think 5 .30 is about right. 5 .30 is 6 o 'clock.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, he's wondering about how big some of these truck cars are. I know he's looking at 40 acres. Most of the truck car is around about 40 acres. The big ones are 100 acres.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Do you mean all by hand?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: At that time, they don't have any... I know, those are the ones that have 15 or 18 people working, you know. They work 12, 14 hours a day. Yeah, sure. Yeah, that's right. The big one, the big farm, they got 100 to 105 somewhere around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they sell the produce?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Most of the produce was in Walla Walla, you know, Pacific fruit and Walla Walla produce and all those things.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Although every day they go and have a little pickup, a little horse cart with a little...you know, it's like a little pickup. They go peddle them from house to house. They have regular customers. They come right out to the front door, you know, and pick their vegetables and pay them and do that. They used to be in Lewiston. When you go down Lewiston, you ask them now. There wasn't too many years ago that a Chinese man there with two big baskets had vegetables. In a way, he peddled from house to house. It's stuck with a pole on the shoulder. It goes up and down the street. You ask Ted Lloyd to let Ted Lloyd tell you. See, what he called it. It's very interesting. I never seen nothing more like that. You know, I seen them in the truck garden. They carried from there to home and then loaded on the wagon. But this guy here in Lewiston, he had a pole he carried from house to house with a peddle.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So when you first came there, you didn't do any work. You just went to school. Oh yes, I did work. I worked for a dollar a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I was in high school at that time. No, I was in seventh grade. Well, you see, at that time my father had to get out of the business there and into a restaurant business. He had to get out of the business with three other partners. And of course the three other partners hadn't got any children. See, they haven't got any children. In other words, my father had to feed me some. Some of them don't like it. So I had to go get myself a job. So I got myself a job with some insurance lady. An insurance man and his wife. I worked for a dollar a week. Do all the housework. Do all the housework. Do all the cleaning up. Not most of the cooking, but I helped cook. And start the old Model T Ford in the morning. I had to boil water to pour on the radiator in the wintertime to keep it warmed up. So when you crack it, it'll go. Otherwise the freezer won't start. Then we have to jack up the high wheel so it'll be easier to turn. The Model T Ford unit, those are out of your... I've seen one running down the street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You jack up those wheels?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You jack up one wheel. You jack up the high wheel. Because when you crack it, it's a lot easier than when you're in the winter. Then that one wheel will turn. You crack it a lot easier and it's all on the ground. You turn the engine that way. It's a lot easier if you jack up one wheel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you put a stick? You put something in there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Jack on that. No, you put something in there to crank it.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You put a hole in there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, those are already there. Those old models. They already have stickers. They get like a tire thing. You fix it on a tire. I saw those. I didn't see them there. It comes out with a little thing that way. Oh, they're always stuck on the car. They used to. The older ones are stuck on there. They hold on to it and then you just push it in and crack it and it comes out. That's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked for these people for a dollar a week, how did they treat you?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I would consider that I wouldn't like it. That's why sometimes I hate to say these things. I didn't like it at all very well because in the first place they don't give me enough to eat. I'm hungry all the time. Because these two people have no children. They're just a man and a wife. Their name is Winterburn. They're just a little bit less dangerous. They have no children and that's all he sells is insurance. He's just an insurance salesman for New York Life.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: She is a social type of woman. Oh, that's okay. They're undoubtedly dead by now. Oh, yeah, they're dead by now. They're social climbers. Oh, she's terrible. In Walla Walla there are two groups of people. There's the Ankenies and the Marshalls. Those are the wealthy people. Those are the wealthy rich farmers. Then there's another group that are quite wealthy too, but this group smokes. This is a social group that likes to drink and smoke more. There's another group that doesn't quite do that way. She's trying to get in both of them and therefore she'll put on a party maybe once in a month or so. Then for the next week or ten days we don't get nothing to eat practically. And for them, the older people, it's alright. The trouble is I just eat whatever is left of their way of coffee. Of course he doesn't get to share the family. I eat in the kitchen, so whatever got left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they deprive themselves of food after a party like this?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They were trying pretty hard to get a party. That's why I hate the business. It just reminds me exactly of what people remember.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: There's a couple that I know, well now they live near Princeton, and she grew up near Harvard. That's where she grew up. To go to high school, the only high school was in Potlatch. To go to high school there she had to work in town so she could live there. They had no way of getting there. Well actually it was Harvard so it was a little further. So she had to live with the families on Knob Hill in Potlatch because they were the people that had the hired help. Every better family on Knob Hill had a servant girl from the country. She said to me there were times when she didn't have enough to eat. It depended on the families. Some families worked more than others. But the ones that some were just so...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: To have a hired help was a sign of their success. That's exactly the same way. Everybody in Walla Do Do, those big rich people, Knob Hill people in Walla Do Do, they all got Chinese cooks. But this poor lady here, she got to have a Chinese cook also. So she hired me for a dollar a week. That's the same thing, no food. We like to say when they have dinner, boy her husband don't like to talk because when they have dinner they have to clean the house and do this. And you got to get her hair fixed. You know he's a poor insurance salesman. Where those other guys got Mormon cars and big cars over here, he got a little while before he tried to climb up with them. It's terrible. In the morning like I said it's just a piece of toast and maybe one egg. Sometimes you don't get an egg. But when we have parties and got lots of food for that one day, that's all. And after that you don't get an egg for a week. A week or ten days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you used to go to school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah I used to go to school. I used to go to school because then I had to go home to get something to eat sometimes. I'd be so hungry I had to go home and get something to eat.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And you stayed at home?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No I stayed at their house. That's part of it. I stayed at their house. Oh so but every day, almost every so often you have to go home and get something to eat. Because I stayed at their house because in the morning I had to go build a fire in the furnace. And I'd say into the furnace chop the wood and take the ashes out and all that stuff too. And he goes to work about 8 o 'clock. He's not eating enough bread to fill him up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they talk to you very much?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh they're really nice people. They're really nice, awfully nice people as far as I can tell. But like I said they haven't got the money, they're just social climate. They're just trying to get up with the young. She's more that way. He doesn't want to be that way but she's that way so you can't do anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did you stick with them?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh it must be a year and a half or two. I got out of the eighth grade. I got out of the shark scene school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like when you first started school there and you were a six -year -old?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: When you started you didn't know much English. About seven before. You don't know a word of English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it to learn?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It's rough. But of course as a general rule the teachers are very good to the Chinese people. They're willing to stay after school at 2 .30, 3 o 'clock. They'll work an hour with you. They'll help you a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you've got the personal education?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah you do. All the Chinese avoid that way. In fact they all do that. Some of them are still alive. Some once in a while they'll teach you. They know it's a long time back. And also the church helps out a lot too. The church would have a lady that maybe teach a whole group of kids at the same age. In the evening school. They help them get started. They're very good that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you get through first grade with all the kids and second grade too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah. You went right through. You went through with them.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Went through high school. They went through high school and they didn't want to go forward.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take very long for most of the kids there to accept you as being just another kid? Or did they warm up to you right away? Or did it depend on the kid?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It depended really on the kid. You never did get all warm up. Well all everybody knows it didn't like that. Like I said there's always a certain amount that... The boys are a little bit better. You get friendly but the girls just stay away entirely. Of course you know, not having a home or a mother. Yeah that's right. It might make it a little bit different too. He can have a boy come up to play with him. He maybe just have one bedroom with his dad. No home life. What you call a birthday party or something. He never had a birthday cake until after we were married or so.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It's a little bit more difficult that way for them to adjust and for the other kids too to adjust to him. When Gene Settle and I were talking he was telling me how it was really different when he went out in country school. It was a lot easier. Oh yeah a little easier. Yeah that's right. That it was when he came into town. At least at the beginning it was harder. And you went to school right in the town. Yeah right. Baker school. I went to the Baker school.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Right in the town. Well and when they are young too. That's not too bad. Yeah not too bad. In the first grade they went young. The only kids were fire secure. Especially the teachers. Usually the teachers are not... They are with you with these new kids. They try to make it easy for you to transition into... Well I know when I came over I was 10 years old. Ten years old when you came to the United States. When I came to the United States. I started with the first grade but it didn't take me very long to... As soon as I get known of English I can go on that thing.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You mean you didn't... I started with the first grade.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well it took me five years, five or six years to finish the first eighth grade. But the first few grades I... because I always had some English... some history and geography and math. Especially you know in China already. So all I need was English and that's what he needs. Of course he needs everything then. At that age. He never had been to school. But I had already been to Chinese school five years. So I know everything except English. So it's easier for me to adjust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when your parents decided to come over were the reasons much like why his father decided to come?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes to get a better you know... A living. A living. Then back in China it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did your mother come too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No my father was over here first.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He came over here by himself first and that was right after they were married. And then he came over and spent a few years and then... Well I was six years old before he went back so the first time he went back it was six years you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first saw him?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah when I first saw him. So it was six years after he first came over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: 1920 when I came over. And then my... well at that... when I was six years old my father went back to China. And he studied a little Chinese medicine then in Hong Kong and then came back over here and it was 1920. So I was ten years old before we came over and so he left us another four years. And of course the second time when he went back then my sister was conceived you see. And so she was four years old and I was ten. I'm six years older than she is. So she and my mother and myself came over 1920 which is four years later on then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it difficult for him to get the whole family over here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yes, it's just as difficult for money matter and for red tape too you see. That's what I was thinking of the red tape if it was hard to get women into the country. At that time he was in business so I was a merchant's daughter and my mother was a merchant's wife when we too came over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he started in business before he never came to the United States?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, he was only a boy when he came over.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I imagine he must have been 17, 18 years old when he came over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think the first time would have been and around when in the year when he would have come over? Was that the first time right after he was married? So that would have been about 1910?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, I was born 1910 and I think that's about five years. No, you were born, oh yeah. Yes, I was born 1910. So I think they were married four years before I... I don't think so. Yes, I'm sure they did. I don't think so. Look, he said he's 20 years older than I am and they were married when he was 16. So at least they'd been married four years, somewhere around four years. So he came over here at 16 or 17 right after he got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it would have been about 1905 or 1906?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: 1904, because 1910... He got married before he came here. Yeah, sure, sure, that's right. Sure, how could they be four years before they were married before you... No, I was born... no, he's 20 years older than I am. So he was 20 years old in 1910. That's when I was born. He was married when he was 16 years old. So I would say 1906 when he came over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then he would have come over when he was 16?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: 16, 17 years old, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By himself?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, he had an uncle. No, his father was older.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, do you know what his father did?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, his father was in the same kind of a store down there in the alley, the Chinese alley there. Supplying the railroad men. Yeah, there are some that come in the store and supply the railroad men. I don't know what he supplied. From that transcontinental railroad. Hope Idaho, all that stuff, the Chinese. That's right, I was reading about that in here, Hope Idaho. Yeah, Hope Idaho, that's right there. That was Hope Idaho, he passed away not very long ago. He was still living there until about five, six years ago. A little bit longer than that. So that's what your grandfather was doing. He was supplying the railroad. He was supplying the Chinese people. Who worked on the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then your father came over and did he work for him, for his father?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I suppose he worked for them and then later on he went to school. Later on he did go to school for a while. Later on he worked in some restaurants too.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Then he came back and stayed for a while and then came back.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, he went back in 1917, 1918 somewhere around there. Hello again. Hi.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You see my father, he came over here a lot earlier than we did. In 18 -something when his father and his uncle.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: When your father was over here though, I guess what happened was he learned to be a merchant. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, see when he went back to China, at the time I was six years old, he learned to be an herb doctor. And so when he came back here, he was an herb doctor and a small care man. He was a merchant in that town. You know, in business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that take much training?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think he was there about three years. Two, three years, something like that. I think he went back in 1917, 1916 I guess, because I was six years old, somewhere around 1916. And then he had two, three years in herb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what kind of training that takes to be an herb doctor? Is it by going to school or do you study with one?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: One knows. Then he gradually, for certain herbs, then you have a doctor, they tell you what they are and you learn that way. You don't have to go that way. But you have to know something how to read. Well, in the old times, the doctor used to be that way too in the pharmacy. You know, you started to be a pharmacy, you go into work in the pharmacy department and then you gradually become a doctor. But that's the thing. The Chinese accepted that way. In the old times, they're still that way, more or less.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he learned to do that and then when he came back with you, is that what he did?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He came back by himself a little bit later on. We came over a year or two later on in 1920 before we came over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have his own store in Spokane when you came? Would it be just an herb store or herbs and groceries?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no. At that time, he was out of the grocery in Chinatown then. He was out when he went back to Hong Kong, went back to China when I was six years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does an herb store carry?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: All kinds of herbs. Herbs, yeah.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And then he would take different kinds of herbs, boxes and boxes of them, jars and glass jars, and roots, and even some little dried animal things too. And for certain things where he would take certain herbs and then cook them and then make a drink out of it and borrow it. That's the way the herbs. It looks like the Indians do a lot of that too. I think in fact maybe Carter still has some of it. They've got a little drawer, you know, where they have different kinds of herbs. They used to have a little drawer, which is the way you call it. The old time. The old drug store. It used to be called the drug store. They would hardly hold that. You get a certain thing and then you... But now they've got all the bottles and pills. And then they stir it. Yeah, they stir it. And then they buy it and then they make it.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But now they've got all the pills. Well, I understand that most of the pills that they use for modern drugs now come out of natural herbs. Yeah, that's right. That's right. They do. They do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did he have the only herb store in China?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, there were two.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: There were two in Spokane. I think there's only one now. One more. I think I've seen that one in Spokane. I think he's on Sprague. I think he moved to Sprague. He used to be on Wall. They used to be between Riverside and Maine. And ours was between Riverside and Sprague. We were up closer to the old John W. Graham up there. That way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he do his business just about completely with Chinese people?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, mostly American people. No kidding. They believe in it. You know, the Chinese herbs, like any herbs, it takes longer to go, you know. With American things, you know, they just shoot it right into the bloodstream. With the herbs, you have to take it through the mouth. And it's not like the capsules either, you know. It takes time to... it goes through as a food. They're slower. Not as slow. A slower process. It's mostly to maintain your health rather than to cure you after you're sick.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They have Dr.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: ... too, doesn't they?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They used to. In the old days. Usually, wherever there's a big enough time, they usually have an herb doctor. They still have some in San Francisco and Los Angeles. They cater a lot to American people too. Did he get his herbs from Seattle or... Yeah, from Seattle or San Francisco. Mostly more from San Francisco. But now it's harder to get now. Especially since it's mostly from communist China. You see, the only place that's not communist China is in Hong Kong. And in Hong Kong, it's just a small island. So most of that. So it has been very expensive and very hard to get some of the herbs coming over. So the herb doctors are really up against it now.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Was it a good -sized... it must have been a pretty big -sized industry in China.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah. They grow any herbs.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: A lot more than the modern doctor, wouldn't you say?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: A lot more. There is only a very small percentage of... Of course, now in mainland China, communist is a little bit different. But it's still more or less...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father do good business?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, he had good business. It's interesting to me that a lot of Americans, you know...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes. They believe in them. At that time. I didn't believe less though now. Because the doctors are...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: what do you call it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They knock against it. They knock against it all the time. Like acupuncture. They knock against it. But now they go like that. acupuncture sure is... They go really like that over in Seattle. Well, heck, even some American doctors are taking courses in China now. Like, actually, our brother, he's going in there and he says American doctors are going in there too. Groups of American medical doctors are going in too, who come to China to take these courses now.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I've heard that in American doctors, that in American medicine they always believe in treating the symptom, whatever that is. And often they don't treat what's underneath it, the underlying causes. That's one of the things they talked about in Chinese medicine. That they treat it... They try to cure you. That's true. Maybe I should be getting going. I think I've stayed quite a while. They want me to... Maybe what we could do...
